subject: catalogDb query fix

catalogDb query fix

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/so-master-verify-java/2085/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/so-master-verify-java/2085

Patch Set 1:

Hi Lukasz, Based on Steve recommendation, can we move forward with just reference data updates to include "*".

Patch Set 1:

A few more questions about this since the intent of the current implementation using * is not clear.

As we know the asterisk character is not a wildcard in SQL, but simply a char. the '%' in conjunction with LIKE clause serves that purpose. 
- So having the asterisk replace all NULL values is just an interpretation and marking of that empty value with something "nice"? 

- How are the asterisks values populated in the DB in the first ? Is it something that must be done manually (by doing the suggested UPDATE) before service instantiation ?
Besides this method, we have not found any other places that rely on the asterisk (neither persisted nor interpreted in the code)

You have mentioned that the UPDATE is the least impacting fix - What would it actually impact (or break) if we simply do not do a select with the asterisk character in the WHERE clause?

Basically if the service type column has to be updated manually each time just to meet the asterisk criteria of the select clause, then it is an issue that should be fixed quickly since it breaks the instantiation flow with an ambiguous, hidden requirement (the update). In this case it may be a cleaner and less complicated solution to rely on NULL values and just ignore the servicetype altogether. The result will be the same - all servicetypes will be considered/included when none are given as criteria. 
Another question/issue (for a future improvement) is if there should be a value introduced for representing ALL service types. This would probably require introducing an aggregated service type value, making the column and entity field serviceType not nullable. It may be a good approach since currently the serviceType values have the following meaning: NULL == '*' == ALL

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> A few more questions about this since the intent of the current implementation using * is not clear.
> 
> As we know the asterisk character is not a wildcard in SQL, but simply a char. the '%' in conjunction with LIKE clause serves that purpose. 
> - So having the asterisk replace all NULL values is just an interpretation and marking of that empty value with something "nice"? 

Yes, it is meant as a more readable field

> 
> - How are the asterisks values populated in the DB in the first ?
repeatable migration script, like all of the other columns.
 Is it something that must be done manually (by doing the suggested UPDATE) before service instantiation ?
No it is in the repeatable script.
> Besides this method, we have not found any other places that rely on the asterisk (neither persisted nor interpreted in the code)

> You have mentioned that the UPDATE is the least impacting fix - What would it actually impact (or break) if we simply do not do a select with the asterisk character in the WHERE clause?
It would break any use case where a service provider used this table without an asterisk and used a service type like "transport' to drive their flow for that specific service_type.  Admittedly this could be solved by a better sql query.

> Basically if the service type column has to be updated manually each time just to meet the asterisk criteria of the select clause, then it is an issue that should be fixed quickly since it breaks the instantiation flow with an ambiguous, hidden requirement (the update). In this case it may be a cleaner and less complicated solution to rely on NULL values and just ignore the servicetype altogether. The result will be the same - all servicetypes will be considered/included when none are given as criteria. 

> Another question/issue (for a future improvement) is if there should be a value introduced for representing ALL service types. This would probably require introducing an aggregated service type value, making the column and entity field serviceType not nullable. It may be a good approach since currently the serviceType values have the following meaning: NULL == '*' == ALL
I am ok if we prefer to write a native sql query.  I think the individual whom implemented this used spring data rest, and probably should of just written a better native query.  I would gladly take a Jira to solve this, but this issue is also appearing in Dublin integration.  I would advise we fix it and improve this in El Alto

Patch Set 1:

Thanks Steve for explanation. Now it is clear what "*" means in this context. I thought that this query is supposed to choose the NorthBoundRequest object regardless the value of serviceType, that "*" means any. But as you said, this query should not choose NorthBoundRequest that has specific value of serviceType, for example "transport". 
So, the "*" in the table means that NorthBoundRequest is for any serviceType. If we put "null" value instead of "*", it would be misleading, because null means that there are no serviceType for this NorthBoundRequest.

In the other hand the current form can be misleading and should be improved later in El Alto release.

Patch Set 1:

Steve/Lukasz, Is there a separate patch that will update the reference data to include '*' for serviceType for Dublin release?
